questions.question: A scam caller shows you a genuine-looking notice with your real PAN number on it. What does that most likely mean?
options: They're actually from a government agency
Your data leaked from somewhere you gave it
questions.reveal: Your PAN sits with dozens of companies. India's PAN was the most targeted identity document globally in 2024.

questions.question: You get a call, a WhatsApp video call, and an official-looking PDF all within ten minutes. Why the multiple channels?
options: Each one makes the next feel verified
questions.reveal: Layered contact manufactures legitimacy. Each channel appears to confirm the others.

questions.question: Which is the strongest reason not to install an APK sent by your bank's official-looking number?
options: Banks distribute only through app stores — always
It might be an old version
questions.reveal: No real bank ever sends an app file. The rule has no exceptions, so you never have to judge the message.

questions.question: Digital arrest victims are often kept on video call overnight. Why does that specifically work?
questions.reveal: Isolation is the weapon. One call to a family member breaks the entire scam.

questions.question: OTP scams in Bengaluru fell by half between 2022 and 2024. What happened to total fraud losses?
questions.reveal: Investment scams rose 1,288% in the same window. Awareness pushed fraud upmarket rather than eliminating it.

questions.question: A Collect request arrives for Rs 1 from an unknown UPI ID. Why send such a tiny amount?
options: To test if the ID works
To get you used to approving without reading
questions.reveal: Small amounts build the habit of approving without checking. The large request comes later.

questions.question: Your phone shows a warning before installing an APK. Why do most victims ignore it?
options: They're already in a panic the scammer created
questions.reveal: Urgency is manufactured first, precisely so safety warnings get overridden.

questions.question: Which target profile does the digital arrest scam actually prefer?
options: People with assets and reputations to protect
questions.reveal: Victims include doctors, professors and retired bankers. Education is not protection here.

questions.question: A wedding invite APK spreads to your contacts automatically. What makes this the most effective delivery method?
options: The victim's trust is inherited by the attacker
questions.reveal: You don't just become a victim, you become the delivery mechanism. Your friends trust you, not the scammer.

questions.question: India's cyber fraud losses tripled in one year. What's the most likely single biggest driver?
options: Scams shifting to high-value, longer cons
questions.reveal: Losses jumped from Rs 7,465 crore in 2023 to Rs 22,845 crore in 2024, while case growth was far slower.

questions.question: A Ponzi scheme pays out reliably for three years. What does that tell you?
options: It's still growing — collapse comes when growth stops
questions.reveal: Payouts come from new entrants. Reliability just means recruitment hasn't slowed yet.

questions.question: A Telegram group gave you five correct stock calls in a row before asking for money. Most likely explanation?
options: Other groups got the opposite calls
questions.reveal: Split-testing con. Send half up, half down, keep the winners, repeat. Survivors think they found a genius.

questions.question: Why do voice-cloning scams almost always involve an emergency?
options: It explains why they need money
questions.reveal: The clone only has to survive sixty seconds. Urgency ensures you never call back to check.

questions.question: Investment scams take roughly three-quarters of India's fraud losses but far fewer cases than phishing. What does that imply?
options: Fewer victims, much larger individual losses
questions.reveal: Roughly Rs 15,000-17,000 crore a year. Fear-based scams get headlines, greed-based scams take the money.

questions.question: A deepfake of a famous businessman endorsing a trading app works mainly because...
questions.reveal: A Pune man lost Rs 43 lakh to a fake video of Narayana and Sudha Murthy.

questions.question: A task-based earning app asks for a small activation fee. What's the fee really for?
questions.reveal: The small payment builds the relationship. It targets students and first jobbers specifically.

questions.question: Why do betting and loan apps demand access to your contacts and gallery?
questions.reveal: That access becomes the extortion tool once you owe them or want out.

questions.question: Someone shows you real withdrawal screenshots from an investment platform. Why isn't this proof?
options: Early withdrawals are paid to attract more money
questions.reveal: Early payouts are the marketing budget. They're funded by the people who come next.

questions.question: Half of UPI fraud victims never report. What's the most damaging consequence?
options: Police can't link cases to find networks
questions.reveal: Individual losses are recoverable. Unreported patterns mean the network behind them keeps operating.

questions.question: What makes guaranteed returns a structural impossibility, not just a lie?
options: Guaranteed return means no risk — which pays nearly nothing
questions.reveal: Risk and return are linked. A genuinely guaranteed return would pay close to a government bond, not 40 percent.

questions.question: Your SIM stops working on a Friday evening. Why does the timing matter?
options: Support desks are closed till Monday
questions.reveal: Attackers pick windows where you can't get help. The weekend is a deliberate choice.

questions.question: In a SIM swap, the scammer needs to fool a telecom employee, not your bank. What does that reveal?
options: Your security is only as strong as its weakest external dependency
questions.reveal: Your bank can be perfectly secure and you still lose everything through a counter at a telecom store.

questions.question: Why is SMS-based OTP considered the weakest form of two-factor authentication?
options: It's tied to a SIM someone else can obtain
questions.reveal: Authenticator apps aren't tied to your SIM, which is why security professionals moved away from SMS.

questions.question: An insider quietly siphons funds for two years. Why do audits usually miss it?
options: They never break a rule they aren't allowed to break
questions.reveal: No control is violated, so no alarm fires. Legitimate access is the hardest thing to police.

questions.question: Forcing employees to take continuous leave is a fraud control because...
questions.reveal: Two weeks away with someone else doing the job, and most long-running frauds unravel on their own.

questions.question: A shopping site offers cash on delivery. Why is that a stronger trust signal than reviews or a padlock?
options: It costs the seller something to fake
questions.reveal: Padlocks and on-site reviews are trivially faked. Agreeing to be paid only on delivery is not.

questions.question: A visa agent charges everything upfront. What incentive problem does that create?
options: They're paid whether or not you get the visa
questions.reveal: Legitimate agents charge in stages tied to filed applications, so they're paid for outcomes.

questions.question: Recovery rates drop sharply after the first few hours of a fraud. Why?
options: Money is split across many accounts and withdrawn
questions.reveal: An Indore professor lost Rs 33 lakh and recovered Rs 26.45 lakh because accounts were frozen fast. Dial 1930.

questions.question: Which is hardest to defend against, and why?
options: Insider fraud — the access is legitimate
questions.reveal: You can block a stranger. You cannot easily block someone you already decided to trust.

questions.question: What single behaviour would prevent the largest share of everything covered today?
options: Verifying through a channel they didn't give you
questions.reveal: Almost every scam depends on you staying inside the channel they control. Stepping outside it breaks nearly all of them.
